summary: Joel Spolsky announces he's moving the Joel on Software translation project from a manually coordinated effort to a wiki-based system. He explains that managing translations in 30+ languages became unsustainable as a centralized operation, costing significant time and money. He humorously admits he was wrong about wikis, having previously dismissed them as vulnerable to vandalism. The new wiki is set up blank and ready for community contributors to translate, organize, and maintain translations collaboratively. He invites anyone—translators, organizers, or even pranksters—to participate.
key_points: Centralized management of 30+ language translations proved unsustainable for one person, even with paid staff support
Joel admits he was completely wrong about wikis, having dismissed them as unworkable due to vandalism concerns
The new approach delegates control to the community rather than trying to coordinate everything through a single bottleneck
He invites contributors at every skill level—translators, wiki organizers, proofreaders—lowering the barrier to participation
The post reflects a broader lesson about trusting community self-governance over centralized control
money_quotes: Sadly, I fell down in face of the effort needed to maintain 30-odd local language versions. Such a job can't be coordinated by one person, especially when that person is me.
I kept saying, "Wikis? Those will never work. Anyone can change the definition of, say, Podcasting, to 'fart fart fart,' and then what happens?"
Lo and behold, I was wrong, Wrong, WRONG.
Speakers of the Flemish dialect ask me to change it one way, then speakers of Netherlands Dutch suggest putting it back, and I have no idea what they're talking about!
If you're feeling naughty, add some fart jokes to somebody's excellent translation. We'll see if the community spots it and fixes it.
Don't wait for me. It's all set up and ready to go. Blank as the day it was born.
key_insight: Some coordination problems that seem to require centralized management are better solved by giving communities the tools to self-organize, even when your instinct says that approach can't work.
